metadata.summary: ADRs capture Context, Decision, Consequences, and Alternatives in docs/adr/ under version control. Accepted ADRs are immutable; supersede with a new ADR rather than editing.

content.title: Architecture Decision Records
content.summary: An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is a short document capturing one architecturally significant decision: Context (why the decision was needed), Decision (what was decided), Consequences (trade-offs accepted), and Alternatives (options rejected with reasons). ADRs are immutable once accepted — never delete or edit accepted content; create a new superseding ADR instead. Store in docs/adr/ under version control alongside the code they affect.

why.p: Architecture decisions outlive the engineers who made them. Without written rationale, teams re-litigate the same decisions, onboard new members slowly, and make changes that violate original constraints. ADRs are also the cheapest way to satisfy "show decision rationale" controls in SOC2/ISO 27001 audits. The key discipline is writing during the decision, not after: retroactive ADRs lose their forecasting value.

list.item: Any technology choice: language, framework, database, cloud provider
Architecture style decisions: monolith vs microservices, REST vs gRPC, sync vs async
Design pattern decisions at system scope: CQRS, Saga, Event Sourcing, API Gateway
Third-party service selection: auth provider, payment gateway, monitoring tool
Breaking changes: API versioning strategy, data migration approach
Security decisions: auth mechanism, encryption standard, compliance approach
Infrastructure decisions: container orchestration, CI/CD pipeline, deployment strategy
Bootstrapping a new repo: ADR-0001 documents the starting architecture
Trivial decisions (file naming, single-developer style choices) — overhead not justified
Reversible cheap changes (variable names, internal helpers)
Throwaway PoCs where decisions don't outlive the demo
Ultra-confidential decisions (M&A, vendor pricing) that cannot live in a public Git repo

rule.statement: Use Nygard format for new teams starting with ADRs — minimal overhead. Use MADR 4.0 when decisions require explicit trade-off documentation between multiple options. Never mix formats within one project.
rule.rationale: Format consistency matters more than format choice. Mixed formats make ADR collections hard to search, review, and tool-process. The team aligns on one format in ADR-0001 and changes it only via a new ADR.

list.item: Nygard (2011): Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences — minimal, fits agile teams, fastest to write
MADR 4.0: Title, Status, Context, Decision Drivers, Considered Options, Decision Outcome, Pros/Cons per option — rigorous, audit-ready, best for complex trade-offs
Y-Statement: one-sentence format "In the context of X, facing Y, we decided Z to achieve Q, accepting P" — useful for ADR summaries but not for standalone records

rule.statement: Follow the ADR lifecycle strictly: Draft → Proposed (team review) → Accepted or Rejected. Accepted ADRs may become Deprecated (still relevant, superseded by newer technology) or Superseded (replaced by a new ADR with a link to the successor). Never delete or retroactively edit an accepted ADR.
rule.rationale: ADR immutability preserves the historical record. Future engineers need to understand why a decision was made, what constraints existed at the time, and when those constraints changed. Editing an accepted ADR destroys this audit trail.

list.item: Draft: being written; not yet ready for review
Proposed: ready for team review; comment period open (typically 1 week)
Accepted: decision is in effect; the team is bound by it
Rejected: decision was considered and explicitly not adopted; record is kept with the rejection rationale
Deprecated: was accepted; no longer actively in use but not superseded by a specific replacement
Superseded: replaced by ADR-XXXX (always include the link to the superseding ADR)

antipattern.description: Deleting or silently editing an accepted ADR
antipattern.reason: Destroys the audit trail; future engineers see the current decision but not the reasoning or the failed alternatives that were considered; creates confusion when the same decision is revisited

list.item: Location: docs/adr/ in the same repository as the code the decision affects
Filename: NNNN-short-title-in-kebab-case.md — e.g., 0042-use-postgresql-for-primary-store.md
Numbering: sequential, never reuse numbers; gaps from rejected ADRs are acceptable
First ADR (ADR-0001): documents the decision to use ADRs and the chosen format
Version control: committed alongside the code change that implements the decision

rule.statement: Commit each ADR in the same pull request as the code change it describes. A PR implementing a new database selection should include both the migration scripts and the ADR. This ties the decision record to the code that implements it in git history.
rule.rationale: ADRs committed separately from their code lose the associative link in git history. When bisecting a regression, the code change and its decision context should appear together in git log.

list.item: New decision: branch from main, add ADR as Draft, implement code, change status to Proposed in PR description, merge when approved
Superseding decision: new ADR references the old one; old ADR updated to Superseded status in the same commit
CI validation: script checks all ADR files have required sections and valid status values (see adr-lint.sh template)
PR template: include checkbox "ADR added/updated if this is an architecturally significant change"

rule.statement: Define who can propose and who can accept ADRs before the first ADR is written. The minimum governance: any engineer proposes; tech lead or designated architect approves; approval requires documented objection handling, not just silence.
rule.rationale: Without governance, ADRs become either bottlenecks (nothing gets written because approval is unclear) or rubber stamps (everything is accepted without review). Document the governance in ADR-0001.

list.item: Proposer: any engineer who identifies an architecturally significant decision
Reviewer: 2+ engineers including at least one tech lead or senior engineer
Approver: tech lead or designated architect for the affected area
Review period: 1 week for non-urgent decisions; 24h for urgent hotfixes (document the urgency)
Objection handling: objections must be addressed in the ADR's Considered Options section, not silently ignored
adr-tools (bash, npryce): adr new "Use PostgreSQL" → scaffolds numbered file; adr list → lists all ADRs
log4brains (Node.js, jhollingsworth): web UI for browsing ADRs; generates static site; MADR-native; CI/CD GitHub Action available
pyadr (Python): CLI for Nygard and MADR; supports status transitions; integrates with pre-commit hooks
ADR Manager (VS Code extension): creates/views ADRs from editor; compatible with multiple formats

rule.statement: Use a four-pass pipeline when asking an AI agent to help write an ADR: (1) trigger detection — identify whether the decision is architecturally significant; (2) context gathering — collect constraints, quality attribute requirements, and existing ADRs; (3) alternatives generation — enumerate 3–5 realistic options with trade-offs; (4) link-back — connect the ADR to related ADRs and the affected code.
rule.rationale: Single-prompt ADR generation produces shallow records that skip alternatives and fail to document the constraints that made this decision necessary. The four-pass structure enforces completeness and forces the agent to reason about trade-offs rather than anchor on the most popular option.

list.item: Pass 1 — Trigger detection: is this decision reversible? does it affect more than one module? does it have a significant performance or security impact? if yes to any → ADR warranted
Pass 2 — Context: what quality attributes are at stake? what are the budget, team skill, and operational constraints? what prior ADRs does this build on or conflict with?
Pass 3 — Alternatives: generate 3 or more realistic options; for each: implementation effort, trade-offs, known failure modes
Pass 4 — Link-back: reference the related ADRs; link to the PR implementing the decision; note which quality attribute scenarios (from the QA methodology) this decision addresses

rule.statement: Always verify AI-generated ADR content for: fabricated benchmark numbers, non-existent tool versions, outdated ecosystem comparisons, and trendy recommendations not grounded in the project's constraints.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate plausible-sounding but fabricated performance figures and cite outdated ecosystem comparisons. An ADR with fabricated data is worse than no ADR — it provides false justification for the wrong decision.

list.item: Fabricated benchmarks: AI may cite "X is 3x faster than Y" with no source — require a linked benchmark or remove the claim
Version staleness: AI training cutoff may be 6-24 months old; verify current version numbers for all tools mentioned
Trendy recommendations: AI anchors on popular patterns (microservices, event sourcing) regardless of project context; explicitly provide your constraints in the prompt
Survivorship bias: AI examples skew toward success stories; require the agent to enumerate failure modes and known limitations for each option
Context injection: always provide existing ADRs, current tech stack, team size, budget, and SLO requirements as input — AI cannot infer your project context from generic knowledge

rule.statement: When capturing any architecturally significant decision (tech selection, style change, cross-cutting pattern, third-party service, breaking API change), kick off a new repo with ADR-0001 from docs/adr/, and run the four-pass pipeline (trigger, alternatives, critic/red-team, link-back).
rule.rationale: ADRs written during the decision — not after — preserve the forecasting value and the reasoning behind rejected alternatives. Retroactive ADRs lose this context.

list.item: Capturing any architecturally significant decision: tech selection, style change, cross-cutting pattern, third-party service, breaking API change
Kicking off a new repo — bootstrap docs/adr/ with ADR-0001 (the architecture you're starting from) so future agents have a baseline
During design phase: agent drafts MADR options analysis from a spec, human picks an option, agent finalizes
Migration / refactor planning: each phase becomes a superseding ADR linked to the original
Compliance/audit prep (SOC2, ISO 27001, FDA): ADRs are the cheapest way to satisfy "show your decision rationale" controls
Onboarding: new contributors read ADL (Architecture Decision Log) instead of pestering tenured engineers

antipattern.description: Write-once-rot-forever: ADRs accepted and never revisited become stale; status fields (Deprecated, Superseded) demand discipline that solo teams skip.
antipattern.reason: Schedule a quarterly ADL review; use a superseder agent that cross-references ADRs vs current package.json / go.mod / Dockerfile / Terraform and flags drift.

antipattern.description: Bikeshedding magnet: PR review on an ADR can stall for weeks while the decision needs to ship — process eats the value.
antipattern.reason: Use time-boxed review periods (1 week standard, 24h urgent) and documented governance so approvals have a clear path.

antipattern.description: Format drift: team starts on Nygard, switches to MADR mid-stream, ends with five formats coexisting.
antipattern.reason: Lock format in ADR-0001; enforce via adr-lint.sh in CI; never change formats without a new ADR documenting the change.

antipattern.description: Missing alternatives: authors list "Option A, do nothing" and "Option B, the thing we already decided." Genuine option exploration is rare without a critic agent.
antipattern.reason: Use the four-pass pipeline with a dedicated alternatives-generation pass; require at least 2 real rejected options documented.

antipattern.description: Decision laundering: ADR is written after the code shipped to retroactively justify it; loses its forecasting value.
antipattern.reason: Treat ADRs as PRs: branch, draft, request review, merge — the ADR PR must precede or accompany the implementation PR.

antipattern.description: No backlinks: ADRs reference code, but code rarely references ADRs — readers find one side or the other, not both.
antipattern.reason: Run a link-back agent that edits touched files to add "# See ADR-0042" comments; include Affects: paths in ADR headers.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.prompt: You are an architect drafting a MADR-4 ADR. Decision needed: <one sentence>. Context: <paste from spec / README / discussion>. Constraints: <team size, stack, budget>. Produce 3–5 considered options. For each: 1-paragraph description, pros, cons, and rough TCO bucket (low/medium/high). Recommend one, explain why. Do not fabricate vendor data; if uncertain, write "NEEDS VERIFICATION".

You are a hostile reviewer of ADR <path>. Identify (a) any alternative not seriously considered, (b) any 12-month risk not listed in consequences, (c) any reversibility cost the author understates. Output as bullet list; max 8 items. Do NOT propose a different decision.

list.item: Bootstrap every new repo with ADR-0001 ("we are starting with monolith / Postgres / FastAPI"); makes future ADRs make sense
Treat ADRs as PRs: branch, draft, request review, merge — never edit accepted ADRs
Cap MADR at ~2 pages; require an "Alternatives" section with at least 2 real options
Use Y-statements as inline annotations (# Y-stmt: in context of …) for sub-decisions that don't deserve a full ADR
Schedule a quarterly ADL review where the superseder agent's drift report is the agenda
Always link ADR to code: ADR header has Affects: paths; code touched by ADR has # ADR-NNNN comment
For multi-repo orgs, keep ADRs in a single docs repo and reference by URL in dependent repos
GitHub Pages + log4brains — Static site, agent-friendly; best practical "ADR portal" — agents push, log4brains rebuilds
GitLab Pages + adr-viewer — Static site, agent-friendly; same pattern, GitLab-native
Notion ADR database — SaaS docs, API yes; OK for non-engineers; risk: drifts from repo, breaks Git-as-source-of-truth
Confluence ADR template — SaaS docs, API yes; common at enterprises; slow, hard to lint, but stakeholders read it
Backstage TechDocs (Spotify) — OSS dev portal, agent-callable via API; catalogs ADRs alongside service ownership
Structurizr — SaaS/OSS, agent-friendly; pairs ADRs with C4 diagrams in one model
Y-Statement annotations (@YStatementJustification) — OSS Java lib; inline ADR snippets in source — agent can scan/aggregate
Joel Parker Henderson's ADR repo — largest public collection of real-world ADRs — reference for agent few-shot examples

template.purpose: Minimal Nygard format: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences
Full MADR 4.0 format with Decision Drivers, Considered Options, Pros/Cons table
Bash script validating ADR files for required sections and legal status values
